spec: Evaluate the cat's proportional presence within the frame. Determine how much of the image the cat physically occupies relative to the total space available. A cat whose body fills a generous portion of the photograph carries inherent visual weight — it is large enough to command attention and difficult to overlook. However, proportional presence is not simply about maximizing size. A cat that is cropped so aggressively that significant parts of its body are clipped out of frame can feel incomplete, as though the viewer is seeing a fragment rather than a whole subject. The ideal proportional presence is a cat that appears both large and complete — generously sized within the boundaries of the image while still feeling fully contained. A cat that is small or tiny within a vast scene scores low on proportional presence (closer to 0). A cat that fills the frame with a sense of wholeness and completeness scores high (closer to 1). A cat that is technically large but visibly incomplete — with ears, paws, or significant body parts cropped away — should score somewhere in between, reflecting that raw size alone does not constitute true proportional presence without the accompanying sense of a fully realized subject.

Evaluate the cat's positional authority within the frame. Determine where the cat is situated relative to the natural focal points of the image — the center, the rule-of-thirds intersections, and the overall visual stage of the composition. A cat positioned at or near these points of natural visual gravity benefits from the geometry of human attention; the eye is drawn there instinctively. A cat placed prominently in the heart of the image radiates intentionality and ownership of the scene. Conversely, a cat pushed to the far edges or corners of the frame — no matter how large — can feel like it is on the verge of leaving the picture rather than commanding it. A cat squeezed into the top margin or sinking into the bottom feels secondary to the space around it. A cat with strong positional authority scores high (closer to 1) — it occupies the compositional ground where the eye expects to find the most important thing in the photograph. A cat with weak positional authority scores low (closer to 0) — it is placed at the periphery, in the margins, or otherwise positioned where subjects of secondary importance tend to appear. This task assesses whether the cat holds the compositional high ground that signals it is the subject of the image rather than a bystander.

Evaluate the visual clarity of the cat as the unambiguous subject of the image. Determine how cleanly the cat separates from its surroundings and whether the environment cooperates in directing attention toward the cat or competes against it. A cat photographed against a simple, uncluttered background — a plain wall, an open sky, a clean surface — benefits from having nothing else in the frame that pulls the viewer's eye away. A cat surrounded by clutter, busy patterns, competing objects, or other visually demanding elements loses authority because the viewer's attention is fragmented across multiple points of interest. Visual clarity also encompasses how distinguishable the cat is from its background; a cat that blends into its surroundings through similar color, low contrast, heavy shadow, or camouflage against a busy pattern is harder to perceive as a distinct subject, even if it is large and well-positioned. A cat with strong visual clarity scores high (closer to 1) — it stands apart from everything else in the image with enough separation, contrast, and freedom from competition to be immediately and unmistakably recognized as the subject. A cat with weak visual clarity scores low (closer to 0) — it is lost in clutter, obscured by competing elements, or blends into its surroundings so thoroughly that it fails to register as a distinct, unambiguous focal point.
